title: Evicted

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine in unison: 'We've paid our rent' / 'Oh, we've paid our rent' — simultaneous defensive agreement
jokes.setup: Landlord arrives to discuss rent, immediately putting the guys on the defensive
jokes.punchline: Both men instinctively say essentially the same thing in overlapping unison, suggesting rehearsed innocence that only signals guilt
jokes.explanation: The overlapping agreement is a nice character beat — these two are so in sync they even protest their innocence together, which is funny because it reads as rehearsed. Functional opener.

dialogue.speaker: Landlord
dialogue.line: They're in new zealand dollars... it's a new zealand account

jokes.text: The landlord reveals he's been accumulating New Zealand dollar checks for two years without noticing they were wrong currency
jokes.setup: The landlord says he collected checks and finally went to the bank
jokes.punchline: He's been sitting on two years of wrong-currency checks — the absurdity is that neither party noticed for that long
jokes.explanation: A perfectly absurd premise: two years of mutual incompetence. The landlord is as oblivious as the tenants. The joke works because it requires everyone involved to be equally clueless, which is entirely consistent with the show's world.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Is that American dollars or real dollars?

jokes.text: 'Is that American dollars or real dollars?'
jokes.setup: Landlord announces they owe $7,727 in back rent
jokes.punchline: Bret sincerely asks whether the dollar figure is in American or 'real' (New Zealand) dollars — revealing he doesn't consider American dollars the 'real' currency
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional line. The reversal — that American dollars are the foreign currency and NZ dollars are 'real' — perfectly encapsulates the show's central joke about these men's total failure to assimilate. It's delivered with complete sincerity (Bret genuinely needs clarification), it's economical, original, and could only come from this specific character in this specific show. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor: sharp, unexpected, highly quotable, perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Can we give you an I.O.U? In American dollars, not in new zealand dollars

jokes.text: The NZ dollar conversion: $7,727 American becomes $77,000 NZ — visual beat where landlord holds up fingers or a figure
jokes.setup: Bret asks if the amount is in American or 'real' dollars; landlord clarifies it's American
jokes.punchline: The NZ dollar equivalent is ten times larger ('...that much. $77,000') — the gap between the currencies makes their incompetence even more catastrophic
jokes.explanation: Payoff to joke 3. The escalation of the number is a good tag, and the implication that these men have been cheerfully writing checks for a fraction of what they thought they owed is funny. Slightly diminished by riding the coattails of joke 3.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Never

jokes.text: 'Can we give you an I.O.U.?' / 'In American dollars, not in New Zealand dollars'
jokes.setup: They have no money; Jemaine tries to negotiate an IOU
jokes.punchline: The landlord immediately qualifies 'not in New Zealand dollars' — the currency problem has now become a running joke requiring explicit contractual clarification
jokes.explanation: Good callback that adds a layer — the landlord has already been burned enough to preemptively specify. Works as a running gag beat.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Never.

jokes.text: 'Can we choose which month?' — Bret asks which month the eviction deadline refers to
jokes.setup: Landlord says 'you have a month to pay or leave'
jokes.punchline: Bret asks if they can pick which month — as if 'a month' is a buffet option rather than 'starting now'
jokes.explanation: A beautifully dumb question that reveals Bret's literal, non-threatening mind. The deadpan 'No.' from the landlord sells it. Economy is very high. Could only come from these characters in this show.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Can we choose which month?

jokes.text: 'Okay, so he's a racist and now you're homeless.'
jokes.setup: Bret explains they're being evicted for paying in NZ dollars instead of American dollars
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately frames currency discrimination as racial prejudice — a completely sincere, completely wrong logical leap delivered flatly
jokes.explanation: Murray's complete failure to understand the situation while arriving at a confident (wrong) conclusion is perfectly Murray. The word 'racist' for currency preference is a funny conceptual leap. Character-specific and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: By whose authority?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: The landlord.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Just because we were paying with new zealand dollars instead of American dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Okay, so he's a racist and now you're homeless

jokes.text: "Evicted? By whose authority? The landlord."
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine recount being evicted
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's indignant 'by whose authority?' is punctured immediately by the most obvious possible answer
jokes.explanation: A clean, brisk joke. Jemaine's affronted question sets up the expectation of conspiratorial injustice; Bret's flat answer deflates it perfectly. Not especially original but well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: To stage your lives as an off-broadway musical

jokes.text: Murray calls a meeting: 'Present. Jemaine. Present. Well, Murray, present.' — roll call with no one absent
jokes.setup: Murray calls an emergency meeting about homelessness
jokes.punchline: He does formal roll call for three people all sitting in a room together, including calling himself
jokes.explanation: Murray's bureaucratic reflex — calling roll for three people who are all right there — is a strong character joke. He can't help formalizing even a crisis. The self-call ('Well, Murray, present') is the best beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Present.

jokes.text: 'Is there a more practical solution?' / 'No, just...' / 'One that relates to our problem?'
jokes.setup: Murray pitches the musical as a solution to homelessness
jokes.punchline: The guys probe for a practical alternative; Murray can't produce one, confirming the musical is genuinely his only idea
jokes.explanation: Bret and Jemaine's double-barreled skepticism ('more practical' / 'relates to our problem') is a good deadpan volley. Murray's non-answer confirms the absurdity. Solid team comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Our story is the story of two guys who start at the bottom and with a lot of hard work continue along the bottom and finally end up at the bottom

jokes.text: Murray describes his musical as 'like Mamma Mia... but this is your musical' — the comparison to Mamma Mia as an aspirational benchmark
jokes.setup: Murray has been secretly writing a musical for two years
jokes.punchline: He invokes 'Mamma Mia' as his artistic touchstone, elevating his vanity project to ABBA-level prestige in his own mind
jokes.explanation: Mild joke — the gap between Murray's ambitions and the actual situation is funny, but 'Mamma Mia' as a reference isn't particularly sharp. Functional character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's a rags-to-rags story

jokes.text: 'It's about a couple of deadbeat guys from New Zealand who have got nothing going on.' / 'Wait, who are those characters?'
jokes.setup: Murray describes the musical's protagonists
jokes.punchline: Jemaine genuinely doesn't recognize himself and Bret in the description — either oblivious or in denial
jokes.explanation: The gap between the obviously accurate description and Jemaine's genuine non-recognition is a clean, well-structured joke. The deadpan 'wait, who are those characters?' is perfectly calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, you say 'really?' here. I knew you'd say that

jokes.text: 'How insulting.' — Jemaine's reaction to learning the musical says 'two hopeless losers'
jokes.setup: Murray confirms 'It says two hopeless losers, yup, that's you'
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is insulted not by the situation being described but by the language used — suggesting he'd prefer more flattering terminology for their identical circumstances
jokes.explanation: Two words, big dignity-gap. Jemaine's misplaced pride is a great character note. He's not upset that it's true; he's upset about the branding.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've only got one bed and I grope people in my sleep. It's a condition

jokes.text: Murray's musical has 'a savvy manager who shows you the light' — 'who's that?' / 'Oh, that's a character loosely based on me'
jokes.setup: Murray describes the uplifting arc of his musical
jokes.punchline: The heroic guide figure is transparently Murray himself — he's written himself as the savior in a story about two people he has monumentally failed to help
jokes.explanation: 'Loosely based on me' is doing heavy lifting — the gap between Murray's self-image and his actual record is a consistent show joke. 'Loosely' adds the right qualifier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's one of the reasons I had to leave the new zealand army

jokes.text: 'Our story is the story of two guys who start at the bottom and with a lot of hard work continue along the bottom and finally end up at the bottom.'
jokes.setup: Murray pitches an inspirational rags-to-riches arc
jokes.punchline: Bret's counter-pitch accurately describes their actual trajectory — a rags-to-rags story with horizontal movement only
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The triple repetition of 'bottom' with escalating specificity ('continue along the bottom… end up at the bottom') is structurally elegant and perfectly describes the show's premise. 'Continue along the bottom' is the killer phrase — it reframes horizontal stagnation as a journey. Highly quotable, original, and entirely character-specific. Earns its 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's actually better for the story, if you're homeless

jokes.text: 'Yeah, it's a rags-to-rags story.'
jokes.setup: Bret describes their bottom-to-bottom arc
jokes.punchline: Jemaine crystallizes it in one phrase: rags-to-rags
jokes.explanation: Four words that land harder than most monologues. 'Rags-to-rags' is an elegant inversion of 'rags-to-riches' that perfectly encapsulates the show's entire premise. Economy score of 10 is justified — it could not be more efficient. Earns its 8.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Your dad?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Yeah. I can't have you at my apartment. What if some crazy shit goes down?

jokes.text: 'Did you see the one about the guys who started at the bottom, stayed at the bottom and at the end they were still at the bottom? So inspirational. Who'd go and see that?'
jokes.setup: Murray mockingly rephrases the 'rags-to-rags' concept — then Jemaine and Bret both say they'd go see it
jokes.punchline: 'I would go see that. It's more realistic.' — they earnestly endorse their own tragedy as compelling entertainment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the premise joke. Murray sets up the absurdity, the guys embrace it with 'it's more realistic' — a meta-commentary on the show itself. The trio working together is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Hotel clerk
dialogue.line: She decapitated him, castrated him, defanged him. She removed his hands and feet

jokes.text: 'Then it's a slow start, isn't it?' — Bret's response to Murray saying 'what if this is just the beginning of your story?'
jokes.setup: Murray offers an inspirational reframe: 'you're at the end of your tether, but what if this is just the beginning?'
jokes.punchline: Bret's literal, flat response punctures the inspirational moment — if NOW is the beginning, the beginning is terrible
jokes.explanation: Clean, economical deflation. Murray reaches for inspirational rhetoric; Bret parses it literally and finds it damning. The word 'slow' is perfectly chosen — not catastrophic, just... slow. Bret's gentle literalism is one of his best character traits and it shines here.

dialogue.line: Oh, that's today's date.
Weird.
Yeah, it's today's date. It happened earlier today

jokes.text: 'I was writing this and at the same time I was reading it. I couldn't put it down.'
jokes.setup: Murray tries to sell the quality of his musical
jokes.punchline: He cites himself as his own enthusiastic reader — the circular self-endorsement reveals he has no actual outside validation
jokes.explanation: 'I was writing it and at the same time I was reading it' is delightful — the tautology passes off simultaneous creation and consumption as a recommendation. Only Murray could offer this as evidence of quality. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: There will be no bringing girls back to the house. I know how you guys are

jokes.text: The musical ends with them staging a successful Broadway musical — 'but that doesn't mean it's gonna happen like that'
jokes.setup: Murray reveals the musical's ending: they stage a Broadway show and it's a great success
jokes.punchline: Bret correctly identifies that Murray has just written wish-fulfillment fanfiction about their future
jokes.explanation: Murray writing a happy ending for their real lives as if that makes it more likely to happen is a lovely absurdist joke. The meta angle — a musical about staging a musical — gives it an extra layer.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Yeah, maybe I'll bring some girls home too, just find some real authentic sluts and just turn this house into a whorehome

jokes.text: Murray shows Bret a scene where he comes into the office and Murray tells him about the musical — 'It's just like now.' / 'Really?' / 'Yeah, you say really? — there you go.'
jokes.setup: Murray claims the musical is accurate because it predicted this scene
jokes.punchline: Bret says 'really?' exactly as scripted, which Murray triumphantly uses as evidence — even though his 'prediction' just caused the line to happen
jokes.explanation: This is a clever bit of self-fulfilling prophecy comedy. Murray creates the very event he's citing as proof — and is genuinely delighted by it. It's a small philosophical joke about causation dressed as character comedy. Well-constructed with a real logical twist.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You must not leave the house.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: When?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What, never?

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Well, not after 11:30 P.M. And preferably never

jokes.text: 'Can we stay at your place tonight, Murray?' / 'Yeah, sure, but I've only got one bed and I grope people in my sleep.'
jokes.setup: Homeless Bret asks to stay at Murray's
jokes.punchline: Murray agrees but casually discloses that he has a sleep-groping condition — delivered with the same casual bureaucratic register as everything else Murray says
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout jokes. 'I grope people in my sleep' is delivered not as a warning or a confession but as a housing condition — like 'the boiler is noisy.' The escalation ('It's a condition. It's one of the reasons I had to leave the New Zealand army') makes it even better. Murray's complete lack of social awareness about what he's disclosing is perfect character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: That is such a womanly instrument. Have you ever heard of a man playing a harp?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: My dad plays the harp

jokes.text: 'It's a condition. It's one of the reasons I had to leave the New Zealand army.'
jokes.setup: Murray has just disclosed he gropes people in his sleep
jokes.punchline: He validates this with a medical label ('condition') and then reveals it already had real-world career consequences — implying he groped enough army personnel to be discharged
jokes.explanation: The military backstory elevates the groping joke from odd character quirk to established behavioral pattern with documented consequences. 'One of the reasons' also implies there were multiple reasons, which is a dark and funny implication. Earns its 8.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I sometimes go to the toilet in the middle of the night.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm. I know

jokes.text: 'We'll just stay on the floor.' / 'I'll probably find you there. I tend to... I really reach out.'
jokes.setup: Bret tries to solve the groping problem by sleeping on the floor
jokes.punchline: Murray warns that distance won't help — he 'really reaches out' in his sleep, as if this is a known spatial issue
jokes.explanation: Good third beat of the groping runner. 'I really reach out' has an almost motivational-speaker quality applied to something deeply inappropriate. The matter-of-fact tone is everything.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Petrov, Yelyena and me / lost but happy at sea

jokes.text: 'Mmm, never mind. No? We'll find somewhere.' / 'That's actually better for the story, if you're homeless.'
jokes.setup: The guys decide not to risk Murray's groping and will find other accommodation
jokes.punchline: Murray's final word on the matter is that their homelessness improves the narrative — he's more concerned with his musical than their welfare
jokes.explanation: Murray finding a silver lining in their suffering because it serves his creative vision is a great encapsulation of his character. The indifference dressed as artistic reasoning is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: So I said, 'do you know any Rolling Stones?' It was a hilarious moment in a very bleak bleak time of my life.

jokes.text: Dave explains he can't host them because his roommate 'is a dangerous drug dealer' — 'Your dad?' / 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Dave says he can't have them over because the other person in his apartment is a dangerous drug dealer
jokes.punchline: The dangerous drug dealer is his dad — a one-word reveal that recontextualizes the danger as also tragicomic
jokes.explanation: The 'your dad?' / 'yeah' exchange is beautifully economical. The deadpan format — question, monosyllabic confirmation — does everything. Dave's dad being a dangerous drug dealer is a funny bit of offscreen character building delivered in two words.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: How come it looks so much like my arm which has been hacked off at the elbow last night?

dialogue.speaker: Petrov
dialogue.line: Oh, it's an arm fish

jokes.text: 'I'm not allowed to have friends over on weeknights.'
jokes.setup: Dave is explaining why Bret and Jemaine can't stay with him; his first reason was 'dangerous drug dealer dad'
jokes.punchline: His secondary reason is that he has a curfew on friend visits — a childlike house rule existing alongside genuine criminal danger
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'dangerous drug dealer' and 'no friends on weeknights' is funny — the mundane parental rule existing in the same apartment as a drug operation. Dave treats both with equal seriousness.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: This seems very similar to 'Star Wars.'

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've mixed you up with... Luke Skywalker

jokes.text: Hotel receptionist: 'We only have one room for $51... room 204. But the thing about room 204 is a terrible murder happened there... today.'
jokes.setup: They have only $51; the receptionist has one room at that price
jokes.punchline: The murder in the room happened 'earlier today' — and the psychologist says the killer will return 'to kill again'
jokes.explanation: The hotel scene is a good extended dark comedy bit. The escalating details (decapitation, castration, 'defanging,' removal of hands and feet) are absurdly specific, and 'today's date, happened earlier today' is a nice deadpan kicker.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Doug and I have something very important to talk to you about. We have decided to spend some time apart

jokes.text: The murder details: 'She decapitated him, castrated him, defanged him. She removed his hands and feet.'
jokes.setup: The receptionist describes the murder in room 204
jokes.punchline: 'Defanged him' — an absurd addition to an already extreme list, suggesting the victim had fangs (or the killer thought he did)
jokes.explanation: 'Defanged' is the standout word in the list — it implies vampire mythology applied to a realistic crime, which is wonderfully absurd. The list structure (decapitate, castrate, defang, remove hands and feet) has good rhythmic escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: This isn't about you.

dialogue.speaker: Doug
dialogue.line: Well, actually, Mel, it is about them

jokes.text: Mel turns out to be the murderer — she appears at the hotel just after the receptionist warns the killer will return
jokes.setup: The receptionist warns the killer will return to the scene — then someone appears
jokes.punchline: It's Mel — their obsessive fan. The scare-chord implication is that she IS the murderer, which also fits her stalker character
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is elegantly set up: murder in the room → killer returns → Mel arrives. The joke works on multiple levels: the immediate scare, the meta joke that Mel's obsession makes her actually plausible as a murderer, and the guys' genuine terror at seeing her. The 'Oh! Shit.' reaction sells it perfectly. Strong structural and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You're so young.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm 32

jokes.text: Mel's rules: no bringing girls home / 'I know how you guys are'
jokes.setup: Mel begins laying out house rules for Bret and Jemaine's stay
jokes.punchline: 'I know how you guys are' — implying she's closely studied their romantic lives, which is both creepy (stalker) and inaccurate (they have no romantic lives)
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy. 'I know how you guys are' from their obsessive fan is creepy, and the irony that they have no romantic history to regulate adds another layer.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: All the professional actors write their lines down on each other's clothing

jokes.text: Doug: 'I don't see any reason why they can't bring girls back here if they want.' / Mel: 'Do you want to bring some girls home too, Doug?' / Doug: 'No, but I don't see any reason why they can't.'
jokes.setup: Mel's rule banning girls is challenged mildly by Doug
jokes.punchline: Mel immediately weaponizes Doug's defense of Bret and Jemaine against him personally — and Doug retreats while still holding his principle
jokes.explanation: Mel and Doug's dynamic is beautifully observed here. Doug has a spine but knows when to pick his battles. His 'no, but I don't see any reason why they can't' — standing on principle while backing down personally — is excellent character writing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: The longer the play, the bigger the shirt. You'll always notice that... big costumes. Why do they always go like this? There's lines covered in all the clothing

jokes.text: Mel threatens to 'find some real authentic sluts and turn this house into a whorehome' if Doug wants to bring girls back
jokes.setup: Doug has said he doesn't see why they can't bring girls home
jokes.punchline: Mel's scorched-earth retort is maximally disproportionate — escalating 'what if they have female visitors' to 'I'll turn our home into a brothel'
jokes.explanation: 'Whorehome' is the word that makes this joke. The invented portmanteau is funnier than 'whorehouse' would be — it sounds more domestic. Mel's nuclear escalation reveals the depth of her jealousy, which is great character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've noticed your acting tends to be a bit boring. It's okay for the first minute, then I kind of drift off

jokes.text: The 'no partying' rule is 'for Doug' — 'He struggled with addiction.'
jokes.setup: Mel lists rules including no partying
jokes.punchline: The rule is clarified as being for Doug, who 'struggled with addiction' — said in front of the man himself as if he's not there
jokes.explanation: Mel casually exposing Doug's addiction history to near-strangers while he clears his throat awkwardly is a good cringe beat. The ahem is the best part — Doug's discomfort reduced to a throat clear.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yesterday's rehearsal you got yourself worked up, you stopped breathing and you fainted.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You were on the ground there for a while, about five minutes.

jokes.text: Rule #8: 'You must not leave the house.' / 'When?' / 'What, never?' / 'Not after 11:30 P.M. And preferably never.'
jokes.setup: Mel has been listing relatively normal house rules
jokes.punchline: 'Preferably never' — Mel's ideal scenario is that Bret and Jemaine are indefinitely imprisoned in her home, framed as a preference rather than a demand
jokes.explanation: 'Preferably never' is an outstanding line. It starts with a sensible curfew (11:30), pivots to 'never leave at all' as a 'preference' — the politeness of 'preferably' against the totalitarian content is perfectly calibrated Mel. Could only be her line. Highly quotable and deeply characterful. Earns its 8+.

dialogue.speaker: Landlord
dialogue.line: Now scram, you filthy illegal immigrants

jokes.text: 'So other than those rules, there are no rules.'
jokes.setup: Mel has delivered approximately 8+ rules including 'never leave'
jokes.punchline: She concludes that these exhaustive, extreme rules constitute 'no rules' — either she considers them normal or she's framing imprisonment as freedom
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the rules sequence. The Orwellian move of declaring the many rules 'no rules' is funny and character-specific. The line works as both Mel being oblivious and Mel being controlling — either reading works.

dialogue.speaker: Audience member
dialogue.line: Whoo! Look at the harp player!

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: It is manly

jokes.text: Mel asks for a song; Jemaine invents 'throat polyps' as an excuse not to play
jokes.setup: Mel asks for a bedtime song
jokes.punchline: Jemaine volunteers Bret's condition ('Bret is overtired and there's a risk of throat polyps') — using Bret as a medical shield while Bret doesn't object
jokes.explanation: Functional joke. 'Throat polyps' is a specific enough fake medical condition to be funny, and Jemaine using Bret as the excuse rather than himself is a subtle character note.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, it depends how you define 'hit.'

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Did people like it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, I don't think it was that sort of hit

jokes.text: Bret claims he can't play because of 'the risk of throat polyps' — then Murray offers to play the harp instead
jokes.setup: Mel wants a song; Jemaine makes an excuse involving 'throat polyps'
jokes.punchline: Murray's solution is to play the harp — an unexpected instrument choice
jokes.explanation: The throat polyps excuse is characteristically oblique (they're avoiding playing for Mel). Murray's pivot to the harp is funny primarily because it sets up the 'womanly instrument' joke.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Unfortunately the play drew a lot of attention to your illegal immigrant status

jokes.text: Doug offers to play the harp — 'Oh, Doug! No.' / 'That is such a womanly instrument. Have you ever heard of a man playing a harp?'
jokes.setup: Mel rejects the guys' excuses; Doug volunteers to play harp
jokes.punchline: Bret's immediate, confident declaration that harp is 'womanly' — a gentle absurdist gender-norm joke
jokes.explanation: Setup for the later payoff at the show's end. Mild on its own but functional. The specific gender-normative objection is funny given the context (two male folk musicians).

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Good shepherding today, guys!

dialogue.speaker: Bret or Jemaine
dialogue.line: Thanks, Murray!

jokes.text: Mel tucks Bret into bed very tightly — 'It's very tight. I can't really move.'
jokes.setup: Mel is being a 'good host,' tucking Bret in
jokes.punchline: The tucking is so tight he's effectively immobilized — Mel's maternal care extends to physical imprisonment
jokes.explanation: Mel's obsessive caretaking rendered as physical comedy. Bret's flat 'it's very tight, I can't really move' against Mel's cheerful 'okay!' is a great cringe-comfort beat. The visual — Bret mummified in sheets — adds to the discomfort.

jokes.text: The heater is broken — but Bret saw her turning it up moments ago
jokes.setup: Bret asks if the heat can be turned down — it's very hot
jokes.punchline: 'The heater's broken.' / 'But I saw you turning it...' — Mel cuts him off and offers to remove blankets instead
jokes.explanation: A tight cringe beat — Mel is gaslighting Bret about the heater she clearly just adjusted. The half-finished 'but I saw you...' is the key moment, and Mel's pivot to 'push the blankets back a bit' as alternative is perfect controlling logic.

jokes.text: Mel locks the bedroom door 'for security' in a 'very dangerous neighborhood'
jokes.setup: Mel is about to leave the room; Bret asks if she can leave it open
jokes.punchline: She locks it from the outside 'for security' — the security is clearly Mel-security, keeping them in rather than danger out
jokes.explanation: The locked door makes Mel's 'preferably never leave' policy literal and physical. The stated reason ('dangerous neighborhood') is technically plausible but obviously not the real one, which is funnier than an explicit villain speech.

jokes.text: 'I sometimes go to the toilet in the middle of the night.' / 'Mm-hmm. I know.'
jokes.setup: Bret protests the locked door by noting he might need to use the bathroom
jokes.punchline: 'Mm-hmm. I know.' — Mel's knowledge of his bathroom habits implies she has been monitoring him long before he moved in
jokes.explanation: 'Mm-hmm. I know.' is one of the episode's best lines. Three words that reveal Mel has been stalking Bret's nocturnal habits — delivered with the same pleasant tone as everything else she says. The economy is exceptional and the character integration is near-perfect: this line could only exist because of Mel's established obsession. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: 'You guys are so cute. I could just eat you up.'
jokes.setup: Mel is leaving after locking the door; she looks at the helpless, immobilized men
jokes.punchline: 'I could just eat you up' — given what we've just seen (locks, heat control, bathroom surveillance), this reads less as affection and more as genuine threat
jokes.explanation: Standard phrase made unsettling by context. Mel saying 'eat you up' to men she has just imprisoned is dark comedy territory. The phrase itself isn't original but its deployment is excellent.

jokes.text: The 'Petrov, Yelyena and Me' cannibalism song — a man stranded at sea with two companions who progressively eat him while he parties with them
jokes.setup: Song begins as a seemingly whimsical sea adventure
jokes.punchline: It becomes clear that Petrov and Yelyena are eating the narrator piece by piece — his leg, then his arm — while he keeps trying to have a good time
jokes.explanation: The best extended set piece in the episode. The escalating cannibalism-as-friendship dynamic is a masterclass in dark comedy songwriting. The narrator's persistent optimism ('lost but happy at sea') against the horrifying reality is tonally perfect FOTC. Key individual gags within: (1) 'he looked suspiciously well-fed' after the leg disappears, (2) the 'arm fish' joke, (3) the Rolling Stones request as a 'last request,' (4) poisoning himself to make himself inedible as revenge. The meta line 'it was a hilarious moment in a very bleak, bleak time of my life' is sublime. This is exceptional comedy songwriting.

jokes.text: THE SONG: 'Petrov, Yelyena and Me' — Bret sings about being stranded at sea with cannibals
jokes.setup: A gentle, folk-style sea shanty begins — the tone is wistful and romantic
jokes.punchline: It is immediately revealed to be a song about being eaten alive by Russian companions
jokes.explanation: The song works as a complete comedic piece: the gentle, pretty folk melody is perfectly wrong for cannibalism. The song earns every dark revelation through careful structural escalation (leg eaten, then arm, 'arm fish' excuse) and the arsenic revenge subplot. Original concept, fully realized. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'I passed out and I woke with one leg. I said... Petrov, have you seen my leg? He said... no. And he went back to bed. But he looked suspiciously well-fed.'
jokes.setup: The narrator drinks all night, passes out
jokes.punchline: 'He looked suspiciously well-fed' — the most delicate possible euphemism for 'he ate my leg,' delivered in the same narrative register as everything else
jokes.explanation: The best single line in the song, possibly the episode. 'Suspiciously well-fed' as a tag for leg cannibalism is an extraordinary piece of comic understatement. The structure is perfect — the missing leg, the denial, then the devastatingly understated observation. Pure craft.

jokes.text: 'It was a hilarious moment in a very bleak, bleak time of my life.'
jokes.setup: The narrator reflects on asking for Rolling Stones during what is actually a cannibalism scenario
jokes.punchline: The self-aware aside acknowledges the comedy while simultaneously acknowledging the horror — 'bleak, bleak' doing enormous work
jokes.explanation: The finest line in the episode. The aside breaks the song's fourth wall to deliver simultaneous comedy and genuine pathos. 'Hilarious' and 'very bleak, bleak' in the same sentence perfectly captures the narrator's psychology — and the show's entire tonal identity. The repeated 'bleak' is the key: one 'bleak' is ordinary, two 'bleaks' is poetry. This is exceptional comedy writing and earns a 9.

jokes.text: 'Hey Petrov, what is that you are eating? It's fish. How come it looks so much like my arm which has been hacked off at the elbow last night? Oh, it's an arm fish.'
jokes.setup: The narrator wakes to find his arm gone; Petrov is eating something
jokes.punchline: 'Arm fish' — Petrov's instantaneous, completely committed lie about a species of fish that looks exactly like a human arm
jokes.explanation: 'Arm fish' is an instant-classic joke. The specificity of the narrator's suspicion ('looks so much like my arm which has been hacked off at the elbow last night') makes the absurd simplicity of 'arm fish' funnier. Petrov commits fully to this transparent lie and it works as both absurdist invention and character beat. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'I swallowed some arsenic to poison my meat.' / 'I was very ill, but revenge is so sweet / unlike the last meal my comrades would eat.'
jokes.setup: Having lost limbs to his companions, the narrator devises a revenge plan
jokes.punchline: He poisons himself to kill them — suffering enormously in the process — and the couplet 'revenge is so sweet / unlike the last meal my comrades would eat' is a perfect dark wordplay payoff
jokes.explanation: The revenge-via-self-poisoning logic is beautifully absurd (he suffers greatly to kill them), and the couplet is a perfect formal joke: 'sweet/eat' as rhyme AND as literal/metaphorical contrast. The concept of 'poisoning your own flesh for revenge cannibalism purposes' is deeply original. Earns 8.5.

jokes.text: 'When I awoke, they were already dead / all that was left of me was my head / No, not dead, just a head.'
jokes.setup: The narrator reports the aftermath of his arsenic revenge
jokes.punchline: 'No, not dead, just a head' — the narrative correction (he's not dead, merely a disembodied head) treats the distinction as meaningful and the situation as survivable
jokes.explanation: The correction 'no, not dead, just a head' is a perfect piece of absurdist deadpan. The optimism embedded in the clarification — as though 'just a head' is a significantly better situation — is the joke. The rhyme (dead/head) and the rhythm are excellent. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Lost but so lonely... at sea.' — the final couplet of the song shifts from 'lost but happy' to 'lost but so lonely'
jokes.setup: The song began with 'lost but happy at sea'
jokes.punchline: The ending quietly swaps 'happy' for 'so lonely' — the narrator is now a disembodied head with no companions, which is technically a resolution but an awful one
jokes.explanation: The callback to the opening lyric with the single word changed ('happy' → 'so lonely') is structurally beautiful. The song has moved from companionship to isolation — and the isolation is achieved by being eaten. The tonally muted ending (no triumph, just a lonely head) is pitch-perfect FOTC.

jokes.text: Murray's musical rehearsal reveals he's cast actors to play Luke Skywalker's aunt and uncle — 'This seems very similar to Star Wars.'
jokes.setup: Murray directs the 'authentic' backstory scene for Bret's New Zealand farm life
jokes.punchline: He's accidentally staged the opening of Star Wars with real actors, then has to dismiss them
jokes.explanation: Murray's plagiarism is funny because he's completely unaware of it. 'I've mixed you up with Luke Skywalker. Damn it.' is a great character beat — he's confused two real people with a fictional one. The casualness of 'sorry guys, you won't be needed for this scene' to the Star Wars actors is excellent.

jokes.text: 'I thought you were a farmer.' / 'Never ever a farmer.' / 'I was a shepherd.' / 'A shepherd, not a farmer.'
jokes.setup: Murray has written Bret's backstory incorrectly; tries to course-correct
jokes.punchline: Both Bret and Jemaine are shepherds, not farmers — Murray has been managing them for years and doesn't know this
jokes.explanation: Murray's ignorance of his clients' actual backgrounds is a good character joke. 'Both shepherds? That's how we met — when Jemaine lost some sheep and they wandered into my paddock' adds unexpected pastoral specificity that makes their friendship origin story charming and funny.

jokes.text: Jemaine and Bret met because Jemaine lost some sheep that wandered into Bret's paddock
jokes.setup: Murray has learned they were both shepherds
jokes.punchline: Their origin story is that they became friends through a stray sheep incident — an unexpectedly wholesome and pastoral backstory for two struggling NYC musicians
jokes.explanation: The friendship origin story is endearing and funny. The image of these two men in New York tracing their partnership back to a lost sheep is gently absurd and very FOTC. Good character-building joke.

jokes.text: 'What about the wise old guy you had to find who taught you to fight your father?' / 'I think that's Star Wars.'
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to salvage the non-Star Wars elements of his script
jokes.punchline: He's included Obi-Wan Kenobi and the Darth Vader plot — the Star Wars plagiarism goes much deeper than the aunt and uncle
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Star Wars gag. 'Fight your father' reveals Murray has essentially written all three acts of Star Wars without noticing. 'I think that's Star Wars' delivered flatly by Bret is the right straight-man response.

jokes.text: Mel and Doug announce a separation — delivered to Bret and Jemaine as if to children: 'This isn't about you' / 'Well, actually, Mel, it is about them.'
jokes.setup: Mel and Doug call a serious meeting
jokes.punchline: They frame the separation in the language of explaining divorce to children, complete with 'we both love you very very very very very much' — treating adult men as their children
jokes.explanation: The 'telling the kids about the divorce' framing applied to Bret and Jemaine is an excellent sustained bit. It works on two levels: Mel's maternal obsession made explicit, and the guys playing along (slightly) as if they ARE the children. 'This isn't about you' / 'Well, actually it is about them' — Doug's honesty breaking the script is the best beat. Earns 8+.

jokes.text: "This isn't about you." / "Well actually, Mel, it is about them."
jokes.setup: Mel delivers the classic divorcing-parent line 'this isn't about you'
jokes.punchline: Doug undercuts it — it IS about them, they're the reason for the separation arrangement
jokes.explanation: A precise subversion of the cliché — Mel delivers it reflexively, Doug accidentally tells the truth. The 'don't say that in front of them, it's damaging' response is perfectly in the bit.

jokes.text: 'Do not say that in front of them, Doug. It's damaging.'
jokes.setup: Doug correctly identifies that the separation IS about Bret and Jemaine
jokes.punchline: Mel treats the honesty as psychologically damaging to the 'children' — the parenting metaphor fully committed to
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the parenting bit. Mel's concern about psychological damage to adult men who are not her children is perfectly executed. 'It's damaging' is the right register.

jokes.text: Mel assigns custody: Bret stays with Mel, Jemaine goes with Doug — 'But I will fight you, Doug, and I will get you back, Jemaine.'
jokes.setup: Mel explains the living arrangements post-separation
jokes.punchline: She announces a custody battle for Jemaine — treating a grown man as a shared dependent worth fighting over
jokes.explanation: The custody battle escalation is the peak of the divorce-framing bit. 'I will fight you, Doug' is funny because it's simultaneously melodramatic and petty — and because Doug barely reacts. The assignment of specific band members to specific parents adds a good absurdist layer.

jokes.text: 'Oh, Bret, you'll understand when you're older, okay? You're so young.' / 'I'm 32.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks why they can't all stay together
jokes.punchline: Mel condescends to him as if he's a young child; Bret's flat '32' punctures the illusion
jokes.explanation: Perfect one-two. Mel's 'you're so young' sets up a specific expectation that Bret is a child or teenager; his flat '32' destroys the illusion completely. The joke is in the gap between the condescension and the reality. Economical and punchy. Earns 8.

jokes.text: 'Now, Jemaine, don't worry. You'll still see a lot of me.' / 'Mmm, um, yeah, probably.'
jokes.setup: Mel reassures Jemaine about the custody split
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's 'Mmm, um, yeah, probably' is the most non-committal possible response — he can't bring himself to say he wants to see Mel, but he knows she'll find him anyway
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's reluctant 'probably' acknowledges that Mel will be wherever he is regardless of living arrangements. Good character beat delivered with the right amount of resigned discomfort.

jokes.text: 'Mel... are you wearing my shirt?' / 'Yes, I am.'
jokes.setup: Mel is reassuring Bret he'll still see Doug after the split
jokes.punchline: Bret notices Mel is wearing his shirt — unanticipated intimacy that implies she's been in his belongings
jokes.explanation: The shirt discovery is a quiet revelation — Mel has already infiltrated Bret's wardrobe and is wearing his clothes. 'Yes, I am.' with no explanation or apology is perfect. The three-word confirmation is more unsettling than any excuse would be. Excellent character integration, good economy.

jokes.text: Murray's cast meeting — actors writing lines on each other's clothing because 'all professional actors do it'
jokes.setup: Murray asks how the guys will remember their lines
jokes.punchline: Professional actors write lines on each other's clothing, according to Murray — an entirely fabricated 'fact' he delivers with total authority
jokes.explanation: Murray dispensing fabricated theatrical knowledge with complete confidence is classic Murray. 'You'll always notice that... big costumes. Why do they always go like this? There's lines covered in all the clothing' — the improvised logic supporting the false premise is beautifully extended. The demonstration of checking lines by going in close to read another person's shirt is a visual comedy gold.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Bret, you've got to remember to keep breathing the whole time. Yesterday's rehearsal you got worked up, stopped breathing and fainted.' / Bret: 'No I didn't.' / Murray: 'You were on the ground for five minutes.' / 'Yeah, about five minutes.' / 'I really got bored of that bit.'
jokes.setup: Murray gives pre-show notes
jokes.punchline: Murray's note about fainting includes the detail that he was 'bored' of watching Bret unconscious — a spectacular indifference to a medical emergency
jokes.explanation: 'I really got bored of that bit' — describing watching an unconscious bandmate as a 'bit' and finding it tedious is exactly right for Murray. Jemaine's corroboration ('yeah, about five minutes') is perfect deadpan. Strong ensemble moment.

jokes.text: Murray's acting note to Jemaine: 'I've noticed your acting tends to be a bit boring. It's okay for the first minute, then I kind of drift off.'
jokes.setup: Murray is giving final notes before the show
jokes.punchline: His critique of Jemaine playing himself ('acting tends to be boring') implies Jemaine is bad at performing his own life story
jokes.explanation: The meta absurdity — Jemaine is a poor actor in the role of himself — is a lovely conceit. 'Kind of drift off' is a funny choice of language for a director's note. Murray's total managerial cluelessness extends to basic performance feedback.

jokes.text: Murray spots Malcolm and immediately abandons the cast meeting: 'Hey Malcolm! Have a good one tonight. You're my favorite actor.'
jokes.setup: Murray has just given Jemaine harsh notes about boring acting
jokes.punchline: He immediately effusively compliments a different actor (presumably in a different show) — revealing his priority isn't his own cast
jokes.explanation: Murray's pivot from criticizing Jemaine to fawning over a passing stranger-actor is a good character beat. 'You're my favorite actor' immediately after calling Jemaine boring is a perfectly timed ironic undercut.

jokes.text: Murray hasn't given them their costumes — 'Hang on! You haven't got your costumes on! What are you wearing? Quickly, get your costumes on!'
jokes.setup: Murray sends them to stage just before showtime
jokes.punchline: He forgot to give them costumes — his last-minute panic reveals the entire production is as disorganized as everything else
jokes.explanation: Standard last-minute-chaos comedy. Functional but not particularly crafted. The character integration is good (of course Murray forgot) but the joke isn't original.

jokes.text: 'Jemaine, nobody wants to listen to the music of a couple of shepherds. I know of a place... A place called... America.'
jokes.setup: The musical begins — the origin story of how they came to America
jokes.punchline: The theatrical portentousness of 'A place called... America' applied to their actual banal decision to emigrate is gently self-mocking
jokes.explanation: The musical-theater convention of the dramatic name-reveal ('a place called... AMERICA') applied to New Zealand shepherds is a good genre parody. The pause before 'America' earns its moment.

jokes.text: The 'America' song — NZ neighbors shouting 'Don't go there! They're crazy! They'll kill youse! They have guns!'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine sing about saving up to go to America
jokes.punchline: The community's warnings ('They'll kill youse! They have guns!') represent genuine NZ provincial anxiety about America, played as counterpoint to the optimistic melody
jokes.explanation: 'They'll kill youse! They have guns!' is a funny distillation of how America reads from rural New Zealand. The 'youse' is perfect NZ/Aus vernacular. The community as skeptical Greek chorus adds musical theater pastiche.

jokes.text: 'Welcome to the land of opportunity / I am the Statue of Liberty... my head's stuck in this chair / gotta get my head out of this chair.'
jokes.setup: The Statue of Liberty appears to welcome them to America
jokes.punchline: The Statue of Liberty immediately has a physical comedy problem — her head is stuck in a chair, and she sings about it — an extremely anticlimactic welcome to America
jokes.explanation: An excellently bizarre theatrical choice. The Statue of Liberty, symbol of hope and welcome, is immediately disabled by a prop malfunction that becomes the song's subject. The specificity of 'this chair' as the freedom-blocking obstacle is wonderfully mundane. Good absurdism.

jokes.text: 'I'm selling myself on the street / just trying to make ends meet / who will buy / my body? / my body.'
jokes.setup: The musical depicts their struggle to survive in New York
jokes.punchline: The song sounds like a proposition but means labor — 'selling myself' and 'who will buy my body' is deliberately double-entendre phrasing for general hustling
jokes.explanation: The double entendre of selling their labor vs. selling themselves is functional but not particularly subtle. Works as a musical parody of the naive-immigrant-in-the-big-city number.

jokes.text: 'Now scram, you filthy illegal immigrants.' — the landlord character in the musical
jokes.setup: The musical recreates the rent confrontation
jokes.punchline: The theatrical version of the landlord adds 'filthy illegal immigrants' — a heightened, melodramatic villain line that also turns out to be accidentally incriminating
jokes.explanation: The theatrical villain landlord calls back Murray's 'he's a racist' read — the musical has validated Murray's interpretation. Also sets up the actual irony: this line performs their immigration status in front of embassy officials. Good structural plant.

jokes.text: 'Illegal immigrants, illegal immigrants / we've overstayed our welcome, we've overstayed our visas / nobody cares, nobody needs us!'
jokes.setup: The musical includes a song about their immigration status
jokes.punchline: They are singing about being illegal immigrants in front of actual embassy officials who were invited by Murray
jokes.explanation: The irony of the 'Illegal Immigrants' number being performed for embassy officials is a well-constructed plot joke. The song is literally a confession. The melodrama of the lyrics ('nobody needs us!') against the real legal consequences makes it land.

jokes.text: Musical-within-musical: Murray's character appears to say 'We'll put on a musical!' as the musical solution within the musical
jokes.setup: In the show-within-a-show, they face the same crisis as in real life
jokes.punchline: Murray's solution in the fiction is also 'put on a musical' — a recursive joke where the solution IS the show we're watching, which contains itself as its own solution
jokes.explanation: The recursive structure — a musical that contains a scene where characters decide to put on a musical — is a genuinely clever piece of meta-comedy. The solution to the problem in the fiction is also the fiction itself. This is sophisticated structural comedy that rewards attention.

jokes.text: Post-show: 'Murray was right. It was a great idea to put on a musical. Murray's the greatest.' — the musical's fictional happy ending momentarily believed
jokes.setup: The show-within-a-show has just depicted their 'great success'
jokes.punchline: For a moment they seem to believe the theatrical ending is real — immediately punctured when Murray confirms the real show got no positive response
jokes.explanation: The characters briefly inhabiting the fiction as reality is a light meta-joke. 'Murray's the greatest' is the giveaway that this is scripted belief. Sets up the real outcome well.

jokes.text: Post-show: Doug plays harp and everyone cheers — 'Look at the harp player! It is manly.'
jokes.setup: Earlier, Bret declared the harp a 'womanly instrument' — now Doug plays one in the show
jokes.punchline: The callback: the crowd's enthusiasm makes Bret revise his position — 'It IS manly' — suggesting social proof is the only arbiter of gender norms
jokes.explanation: The callback closes the harp loop neatly. Bret's immediate reversal ('it IS manly') based purely on crowd reaction is a good character beat. The callback structure is solid.

jokes.text: Murray's post-show review: 'your acting was really good' / 'okay, you're still a bit boring'
jokes.setup: Murray delivers post-show notes
jokes.punchline: He manages to give a compliment and immediately reverse it — exactly as in the pre-show notes
jokes.explanation: A clean callback to the earlier boring-acting note. The compliment-reversal structure is consistent with Murray's character and lands comfortably. Not especially elevated but satisfying.

jokes.text: 'Is it a hit?' / 'Well, it depends how you define hit.' / 'Did people like it?' / 'No, I don't think it was that sort of hit.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks if the show was a hit
jokes.punchline: Murray's distinction — it might be a 'hit' in a different sense (implying failure/disaster rather than success) — is a gentle wordplay joke
jokes.explanation: The 'what kind of hit' misdirection is gentle but well-executed. Murray's diplomatic phrasing ('I don't think it was THAT sort of hit') is good deadpan management-speak for 'catastrophic failure.'

jokes.text: 'I've got lots of news — good news, got some... just a little bit of terrible news there...' / 'Well, let's start with the good news.' / 'Okay. Well, we did it.' / 'That's the good news?'
jokes.setup: Murray promises good news and some terrible news
jokes.punchline: 'We did it' — staging a failed show is the good news; merely completing it is the achievement
jokes.explanation: Murray's 'we did it' as good news is a perfect low-bar-setting moment. The enthusiasm behind 'we did it!' as the maximum positive spin on a disaster is very Murray. 'That's it?' from Jemaine lands well.

jokes.text: The 'illegal immigrants' song drew attention to their illegal status in front of embassy officials — 'It was a good number... but not great to perform in front of these people.'
jokes.setup: Murray invited embassy officials; the show included a song literally titled 'Illegal Immigrants'
jokes.punchline: Murray belatedly recognizes the problem — the song content and the audience were an extremely bad combination — while still defending the number's artistic merit
jokes.explanation: Murray's post-show analysis separates 'artistic quality' from 'suitability for this specific audience' as if they're independent variables. 'A great number... but not great to perform in front of these people' is excellent Murray reasoning.

jokes.text: 'So overall great, yes, but also not great. So average?'
jokes.setup: Murray has established that the show was artistically good but legally catastrophic
jokes.punchline: Bret offers 'average' as a mathematical synthesis — Murray pauses without committing
jokes.explanation: Bret's logical reduction of 'great but not great' to 'average' is a good deadpan math joke. Murray's pause rather than agreeing is the right beat — he can't bring himself to call it average.

jokes.text: In addition to good news and terrible news, there is 'truly awful news' — the embassy officials want to see them
jokes.setup: Murray has categorized news as 'good,' 'terrible,' and now reveals a third category
jokes.punchline: 'Truly awful' — a separate tier below 'terrible,' because the embassy officials (who presumably represent deportation) want to meet them personally
jokes.explanation: The escalating news categories — good, terrible, and now 'truly awful' as a separate tier — is a good structural joke. Murray delivering all of this in the same register makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Final scene: back in New Zealand as shepherds — 'Good shepherding today, guys!' / 'Thanks, Murray!'
jokes.setup: The episode has built to the threat of deportation; the previous scene cut to the embassy officials approaching
jokes.punchline: The final scene is the guys back in New Zealand as shepherds — they've been deported. Murray is also there. The episode's entire arc ends with everyone back at square one, with Murray still managing them as shepherds.
jokes.explanation: The perfect episode-closing joke. The entire season's arc of failing in New York ends with deportation back to New Zealand — the rags-to-rags story complete. Murray being there as their shepherd-manager implies he was deported too, or followed them, either of which is funny. 'Good shepherding today, guys!' Murray's cheerful continuation of management in a sheep paddock is the ideal final image. The economy is extraordinary — this single scene resolves the entire episode's tension and pays off the rags-to-rags joke, the shepherd revelation, and the deportation threat in six words. Series finale energy. Earns 9+.

tmdb_overview: Bret and Jemaine can't afford to pay their rent and are forced to move in with Mel and Doug, and Murray believes they can make some money with a theatrical production about the band.
